rate_limiter: Break cost_uniform: price each request by its measured resource footprint instead of counting it as one token. The gateway charges a weighted cost (CPU-ms, bytes egress, downstream calls fanned out) computed from a live cost model, and the token bucket drains by that weight. A cheap GET and an expensive aggregation query no longer count the same, so a tenant cannot starve the backend with a few heavy calls while staying under a naive QPS cap.

Break tenant_independent: replace per-tenant buckets with a single shared pool allocated by max-min fairness. Idle tenants' unused capacity is dynamically lent to active ones, but a tenant's guaranteed floor is reclaimed instantly when it returns. This couples tenants on purpose: total throughput rises during off-peak skew, yet no tenant can be permanently squeezed below its reservation.

Break tenant_independent: model tenants as a hierarchy and enforce limits at every level (org -> team -> key). A noisy key is throttled against its team's budget before it can consume the org's share, and weights propagate downward via hierarchical token buckets (HTB-style). This stops a single compromised credential inside a big customer from cannibalizing the whole tenant's quota.

Break time_windowed: abandon fixed/sliding windows for a continuous virtual-time scheduler (deficit round robin / WFQ). Each request is stamped with a virtual finish time and admitted in that order, so admission is decided by relative fairness at the instant, not by which window boundary it landed near. This removes the classic burst-at-window-edge double-spend without tuning window size.

Break time_windowed: make the limit reactive to backend health rather than wall-clock windows. An AIMD/adaptive controller raises the effective rate while p99 latency and error rate stay healthy and multiplicatively backs off when the downstream shows stress. The 'limit' becomes a closed-loop signal tracking real capacity instead of a static per-second number that is wrong whenever load shifts.

Break time_windowed: schedule on predicted future load with a token reservation/credit-banking scheme. Tenants accrue credits during quiet periods and may spend a bounded reservoir during bursts, while a short forecast reshapes admission to pre-empt collisions. Time stops being a memoryless reset and becomes an account with savings and overdraft limits, smoothing bursty traffic without dropping legitimate spikes.
cache: Break cost_uniform: weight cache admission and eviction by the cost-to-recompute, not by hit frequency alone. A GDSF/cost-aware policy keeps entries whose miss is expensive (slow query, large fan-out) even if accessed less often, and evicts cheap-to-refetch entries first. Fairness then means equalizing recompute-cost saved per tenant rather than equalizing slot counts.

Break tenant_independent: partition the cache with shared-but-bounded segments using a global max-min allocator over capacity. Idle tenants' unused cache lines are loaned to bursting tenants, but each tenant has a reserved floor it can reclaim on its next miss. One tenant's burst can no longer evict another tenant's working set below its guarantee.

Break tenant_independent: deduplicate identical or overlapping entries across tenants and account the shared cost fairly. When tenants request the same upstream object, store one copy and split its 'rent' via Shapley-style attribution, so a popular shared key is not billed entirely to whoever happened to miss first. This couples tenants to raise effective hit rate while keeping cost accounting fair.

Break time_windowed: replace TTL-by-clock with TTL-by-access-recency and staleness budget. Entries carry a freshness contract; serve-stale-while-revalidate kicks in based on observed read patterns rather than a fixed expiry, and revalidation is rate-limited per tenant. Cache validity is governed by usage and tolerated staleness, not by a uniform wall-clock window that expires hot keys at the worst moment.

Break time_windowed: drive admission with a continuous popularity estimator (TinyLFU/count-min sketch with aging) instead of windowed frequency counts. The sketch decays smoothly, so a tenant's burst is admitted on its true rising frequency, not on which counting window it fell into. This avoids cold-start unfairness where a new bursty tenant loses to a stale window's incumbents.

Break time_windowed: prefetch and warm against a short load forecast so bursts hit warm cache. A predictor flags keys likely to spike per tenant and pre-populates them during idle capacity, with a fairness governor capping prefetch budget per tenant. The cache stops reacting one-window-late and instead schedules warmth ahead of predicted demand, smoothing the burst before it arrives.
convergence: Break gradient_informative: prove convergence using only a stochastic zeroth-order estimate of the gradient. Build a finite-difference / Gaussian-smoothing surrogate and show the method reaches an epsilon-stationary point of the smoothed objective, with rate degrading polynomially in dimension. The theorem certifies progress when the true gradient is unavailable or untrusted, relying on smoothing-induced descent rather than the exact gradient's information.

Break gradient_informative: assume the gradient is corrupted by adversarial/heavy-tailed noise and prove convergence under a robust aggregation step. Replace the mean update with a median-of-means or clipped estimator and show that, despite a constant fraction of arbitrarily wrong gradient samples, iterates still reach a neighborhood of a stationary set whose radius scales with the corruption fraction. Informativeness is replaced by a robustness budget.

Break gradient_informative: use sign-only or quantized gradient information and prove a convergence guarantee. With just the coordinate-wise sign (signSGD), show convergence to a stationary point under a separable-smoothness condition, with rate governed by the gradient's variance-to-magnitude ratio. The theorem reframes 'informative' as merely directional: the magnitude is discarded, yet descent persists.

Break smoothness_required: drop Lipschitz-smoothness and prove convergence under weak-convexity / prox-regularity using the Moreau envelope. Show the envelope's gradient norm decreases at a quantified rate, certifying near-stationarity for objectives with kinks (ReLU-type, max-of-smooth). This handles non-differentiable non-convex landscapes that classical L-smoothness theorems exclude entirely.

Break smoothness_required: assume only relative smoothness w.r.t. a Bregman reference function and prove mirror-descent convergence. Where the Euclidean gradient blows up (e.g. self-concordant or exponentially-growing objectives), choosing a matched mirror map yields a clean descent inequality and an O(1/k) stationarity rate. The theorem trades a global Lipschitz constant for a problem-adapted geometry.

Break static_landscape: prove a tracking guarantee for a time-varying non-convex objective. Bound the path-length / drift of the stationary set between steps and show the iterates stay within a quantified tube of the moving stationary point, with steady-state error scaling with the per-step drift. Convergence becomes 'tracking a moving target' rather than reaching a fixed minimizer, relevant to online and streaming optimization.
matchmaking: Break evaluation_is_fixed: stop scoring matches only by post-hoc win/loss and instead evaluate fairness causally via controlled interleaving experiments. Run A/B matched cohorts where the matchmaker's intended balance is the treatment, and measure churn, comeback rate, and self-reported enjoyment as the real objective. The evaluation metric becomes an experimentally validated outcome, not a static rating-gap proxy that may not correlate with felt fairness.

Break objective_is_given: optimize for match quality and engagement instead of pure skill-balance. Define the objective as maximizing the probability of a 'close, non-toxic, fun' game (predicted blowout probability, queue time, smurf risk) and trade off skill symmetry against these. Fairness is redefined as 'everyone has a good time and a chance', so deliberately uneven-skill matches that play out closer can be preferred.

Break objective_is_given: make the objective multi-stakeholder and explicit via a welfare function over players, not a single per-match metric. Jointly minimize variance in long-run win rate across the whole population while bounding individual queue time, using a constrained optimization (e.g. maximize Nash social welfare of experience). The system targets population-level fairness over a season rather than balancing each game in isolation.

Break representation_is_fixed: replace the scalar Elo/MMR with a multidimensional skill vector (aim, macro, role, tilt-resistance) and match on vector compatibility. Two players with equal scalar MMR but orthogonal strengths can be paired complementarily, and matches balance the team skill profiles, not just totals. Richer representation exposes mismatches a single number hides.

Break representation_is_fixed: model skill as a probabilistic latent (TrueSkill-style Gaussian, or a learned embedding with uncertainty) and match on full distributions. New or volatile players carry wide uncertainty and are matched to limit downside risk, while convergence reduces variance. Matchmaking optimizes over belief distributions rather than point estimates, handling cold-start fairness directly.

Break representation_is_fixed: represent players as nodes in a relational graph (party, history, social ties, recent teammates) and match via graph structure, not isolated ratings. A GNN embedding captures synergy/anti-synergy and avoids repeatedly pairing the same toxic combinations. Identity becomes contextual and relational, so the matcher can optimize team chemistry and rematch-avoidance that a per-player scalar cannot express.
